subject: Physics
q:
The relationship between the magnetic susceptibility $(\chi)$ and the magnetic permeability $(\mu)$ is given by :
($\mu_0$ is the permeability of free space and $\mu_{\mathrm{r}}$ is relative permeability)
question:
The relationship between the magnetic susceptibility $(\chi)$ and the magnetic permeability $(\mu)$ is given by :
($\mu_0$ is the permeability of free space and $\mu_{\mathrm{r}}$ is relative permeability)
solution:
We have
$\begin{aligned} & \mu_{\mathrm{r}}=(1+\chi) \Rightarrow \chi=\left(\mu_{\mathrm{r}}-1\right) \\ & \mu=\mu_0 \mu_{\mathrm{r}} \Rightarrow \mu_{\mathrm{r}}=\frac{\mu}{\mu_0} \\ & \therefore \chi=\left(\frac{\mu}{\mu_0}-1\right)\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A paramagnetic substance in the form of a cube with sides $1 \mathrm{~cm}$ has a magnetic dipole moment of $20 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{T}$ when a magnetic intensity of $60 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{m}$ is applied. Its magnetic susceptibility is:
question: A paramagnetic substance in the form of a cube with sides $1 \mathrm{~cm}$ has a magnetic dipole moment of $20 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{T}$ when a magnetic intensity of $60 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{m}$ is applied. Its magnetic susceptibility is:
solution:
Magnetic susceptibility,

$\chi=\frac{\mathrm{I}}{\mathrm{H}}$

where, $\mathrm{I}=\frac{\text { Magnetic moment }}{\text { Volume }}=\frac{20 \times 10^{-6}}{10^{-6}}=20 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^{2}$

Now , $\chi=\frac{20}{60 \times 10^{3}}=\frac{1}{3} \times 10^{-3}=3.3 \times 10^{-4}$

q: Needles $\mathrm{N}_1, \mathrm{~N}_2$ and $\mathrm{N}_3$ are made of a ferromagnetic, a paramagnetic and a diamagnetic substance respectively. A magnet when brought close to them will
question: Needles $\mathrm{N}_1, \mathrm{~N}_2$ and $\mathrm{N}_3$ are made of a ferromagnetic, a paramagnetic and a diamagnetic substance respectively. A magnet when brought close to them will
options: attract $\mathrm{N}_1$ strongly, $\mathrm{N}_2$ weakly and repel $\mathrm{N}_3$ weakly
attract $\mathrm{N}_1$ strongly, but repel $\mathrm{N}_2$ and $\mathrm{N}_3$ weakly
solution: attracts $\mathrm{N}_1$ strongly, $\mathrm{N}_2$ weakly and Repel $\mathrm{N}_3$ weakly

q:
Hysteresis loops for two magnetic materials A  and B are given below:

These materials are used to make magnets for electric generators, transformer core and electromagnet core. Then it is proper to use:
question:
Hysteresis loops for two magnetic materials A  and B are given below:

These materials are used to make magnets for electric generators, transformer core and electromagnet core. Then it is proper to use:
solution:
As we know that, area of Hysteresis loop represents the net loss of energy by the material per unit volume,taken over complete cycle.


Graph A is hard ferromagnetic material substance as the area of the loop is more, hence the hysteresis loss will be more and thus the value of coercivity will be high which are best suited to use as Permanent Magnet

Graph B is graph of soft ferromagnetic material substance as the area of the loop is less and hence the hysterisis loss will be less which is required condition for transformer. And the value of coercivity will be low too.

Hence this material are best suited to use as Electromagnets and Transformer.

subject: Physics
q:
Two identical small bar magnets each of dipole moment $3\sqrt{5}$ J/T are placed at a center to center separation of $10$ cm, with their axes perpendicular to each other as shown in figure. The value of magnetic field at the point P midway between the magnets is $\alpha \times 10^{-3}$ T. The value of $\alpha$ is ______.
($\mu_0=4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ Tm/A)
question:
Two identical small bar magnets each of dipole moment $3\sqrt{5}$ J/T are placed at a center to center separation of $10$ cm, with their axes perpendicular to each other as shown in figure. The value of magnetic field at the point P midway between the magnets is $\alpha \times 10^{-3}$ T. The value of $\alpha$ is ______.
($\mu_0=4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ Tm/A)
solution:
Let the magnetic moment of each magnet be $M = 3\sqrt{5}$ J/T.

The distance of point P from the center of each magnet is $r = 5$ cm $= 0.05$ m.

For the first magnet (horizontal), point P lies on its axial line. The magnetic field at P due to the first magnet is:

$B_1 = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \dfrac{2M}{r^3}$

The direction of $\vec{B}_1$ is along the axis of the first magnet.

For the second magnet (vertical), point P lies on its equatorial line. The magnetic field at P due to the second magnet is:

$B_2 = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \dfrac{M}{r^3}$

The direction of $\vec{B}_2$ is parallel to the axis of the second magnet, which is perpendicular to the axis of the first magnet.

Since $\vec{B}_1$ and $\vec{B}_2$ are perpendicular to each other, the net magnetic field at P is:

$B = \sqrt{B_1^2 + B_2^2}$

Substituting the expressions for $B_1$ and $B_2$:

$B = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \dfrac{2M}{r^3}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \dfrac{M}{r^3}\right)^2}$

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \dfrac{M}{r^3} \sqrt{2^2 + 1^2} = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \dfrac{\sqrt{5}M}{r^3}$

Substituting the given values $\dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} = 10^{-7}$ T m/A, $M = 3\sqrt{5}$ J/T, and $r = 0.05$ m:

$B = 10^{-7} \times \dfrac{\sqrt{5} \times 3\sqrt{5}}{(0.05)^3}$

$B = 10^{-7} \times \dfrac{15}{125 \times 10^{-6}}$

$B = \dfrac{15 \times 10^{-1}}{125}$

$B = 0.1 \times 0.12$ T

$B = 0.012$ T

$B = 12 \times 10^{-3}$ T

Comparing this with the given value $\alpha \times 10^{-3}$ T, we get $\alpha = 12$.

Answer: $12$

$$
\text { and the cross } \otimes \text {. }
$$
question:
Choose the correct sketch of the magnetic field lines of a circular current loop shown by the dot
$$
\text { and the cross } \otimes \text {. }
$$
solution: If magnetic field is perpendicular and into the plane of the paper, it is represented by cross $\otimes$ and if the direction of the magnetic field is perpendicular out of the plane of the paper it is represented by dot $\odot$.

subject: Physics
q: A bar magnet has total length $2 l=20$ units and the field point $P$ is at a distance $\mathrm{d}=10$ units from the centre of the magnet. If the relative uncertainty of length measurement is $1 \%$, then uncertainty of the magnetic field at point P is :
question: A bar magnet has total length $2 l=20$ units and the field point $P$ is at a distance $\mathrm{d}=10$ units from the centre of the magnet. If the relative uncertainty of length measurement is $1 \%$, then uncertainty of the magnetic field at point P is :
solution: Magnetic field at $P, B=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{m(2 l)}{r^3}$, where $m$ is the pole strength $\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow\left(\frac{\Delta B}{B} \times 100\right) & =\left(\frac{\Delta l}{l}\right) \times 100+3\left|\frac{\Delta r}{r}\right| \times 100 \\ & =1 \%+3 \%=4 \% \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.
Assertion A : Electromagnets are made of soft iron.
Reason R : Soft iron has high permeability and low retentivity.
In the light of above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below.
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.
Assertion A : Electromagnets are made of soft iron.
Reason R : Soft iron has high permeability and low retentivity.
In the light of above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below.
options: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">A</mi></math> is not correct but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">R</mi></math> is correct

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">A</mi></math> is correct but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">R</mi></math> is not correct

Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">R</mi></math> are correct and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">R</mi></math> is the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">A</mi></math>

Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">R</mi></math> are correct but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">R</mi></math> is NOT the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">A</mi></math>
solution:
A coil surrounding a magnetic core having high permeability and low retentivity, through which an electric current is fed to magnetise the core is what makes up an electromagnet. Wherever controllable magnets are needed, as in devices where the magnetic flux needs to be adjusted, reversed, or turned on and off, an electromagnet is used.

Hence, both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A.

q: A paramagnetic material has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>28</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>atoms</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Its magnetic susceptibility at temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>350</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> is <math><mn>2.8</mn><mo>Ã—</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Its susceptibility at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> is
question: A paramagnetic material has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>28</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>atoms</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Its magnetic susceptibility at temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>350</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> is <math><mn>2.8</mn><mo>Ã—</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Its susceptibility at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> is
solution:
From Curie's law, the magnetic susceptibility is inversely proportional to the temperature, i.e.,

χ∝1T

Hence, the ratio of magnetic susceptibility at different temperature T1=350 K and T2=300 K.

χ2χ1=T1T2

χ2=χ1×T1T2=2.8×10-4×350300

χ2=3.267×10-4.

q: A magnetic needle of magnetic moment <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.7</mo><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> and moment of inertia <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mo>.5</mo><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is performing simple harmonic oscillations in a magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. Time taken for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> complete oscillations is:
question: A magnetic needle of magnetic moment <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.7</mo><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> and moment of inertia <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mo>.5</mo><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is performing simple harmonic oscillations in a magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. Time taken for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> complete oscillations is:
solution:
Time period of oscillating magnetic needle is   T=2πIMB

=2π7.5×10-66.7×10-2×0.01 sec

=0.6647 sec

Time for oscillations, t=10 T=6.647 sec⁡=6.65 sec

If it is equivalent to a magnet of the same size and magnetization <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover accent="true"><mi>M</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mi> </mi><mfenced separators="|"><mfrac><mrow><mi>M</mi><mi>a</mi><mi>g</mi><mi>n</mi><mi>e</mi><mi>t</mi><mi>i</mi><mi>c</mi><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi><mi>o</mi><mi>m</mi><mi>e</mi><mi>n</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>v</mi><mi>o</mi><mi>l</mi><mi>u</mi><mi>m</mi><mi>e</mi></mrow></mfrac></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math>then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mfenced open="|" close="|" separators="|"><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></mfenced></math> is:
solution:

Magnetization M→=magnetic monent Volume 

=No. of turns×Current×AreaVolume

=N I AA l

=NIl 

=500×15×10025

=60×500

=30×103

=30000 A m-1

subject: Physics
q: The current required to be passed through a solenoid of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> length and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn></math> turns in order to demagnetise a bar magnet of magnetic intensity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is ________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>.
question: The current required to be passed through a solenoid of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> length and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn></math> turns in order to demagnetise a bar magnet of magnetic intensity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is ________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>.
solution:
The magnetic intensity is

H=Bμ0-M

For M=0, it can be written

H=Bμ0=ni

The data given is 

H=2.4×103 A m-1l=15×10-2 mN=60

Using the relation, n=Nl and H=ni, the value of the current is,

i=Hn=2.4×103×15×10-260=6 A

subject: Physics
q:
An electron with energy 0.1keV moves at right angle to the earth's magnetic field of 1×10-4Wbm-2. The frequency of revolution of the electron will be
(Take mass of electron =9.0×10-31 kg)
question:
An electron with energy 0.1keV moves at right angle to the earth's magnetic field of 1×10-4Wbm-2. The frequency of revolution of the electron will be
(Take mass of electron =9.0×10-31 kg)
solution:
The frequency of revolution of the electron is f=eB2πm, here, e is charge of electron, B is magnetic field and m is mass of electron.

So, f=1.6×10-19×10-42π×9×10-31=2.8×106 Hz

For solenoid, the magnetic field needed to be magnetised the magnet.

⇒I=3 A

subject: Physics
q: The magnetic moment of a bar magnet is $0.5 \mathrm{Am}^2$. It is suspended in a uniform magnetic field of $8 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~T}$. The work done in rotating it from its most stable to most unstable position is:
question: The magnetic moment of a bar magnet is $0.5 \mathrm{Am}^2$. It is suspended in a uniform magnetic field of $8 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~T}$. The work done in rotating it from its most stable to most unstable position is:
solution:
At stable equilibrium $\mathrm{U}=-\mathrm{mB} \cos 0^{\circ}=-\mathrm{mB}$
At unstable equilibrium $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{U}=-\mathrm{mB} \cos 180^{\circ}=+\mathrm{mB} \\ & \mathrm{W}=\Delta \mathrm{U} \\ & \text { W.D. }=2 \mathrm{mB} \\ & =2(0.5) 8 \times 10^{-2}=8 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~J} \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: At an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> to the magnetic meridian, the apparent dip is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo><mo>.</mo></math> Find the true dip:
question: At an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></math> to the magnetic meridian, the apparent dip is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo>°</mo><mo>.</mo></math> Find the true dip:
solution:
Atanδ=tanδ'cosθ

=tan45°cos30°

tanδ=1×32

δ=tan-132

subject: Physics
q: The B-H curve for a ferromagnet is shown in the figure. The ferromagnet is placed inside a long solenoid with 1000 turns/cm.. The current that should be passed in the solenoid to demagnetize the ferromagnet completely is:
question: The B-H curve for a ferromagnet is shown in the figure. The ferromagnet is placed inside a long solenoid with 1000 turns/cm.. The current that should be passed in the solenoid to demagnetize the ferromagnet completely is:
solution:
Given Number of turns, $\mathrm{n}=1000$ turns $/ \mathrm{cm}=1000 \times 100$ turns $/ \mathrm{m}$ Coercivity of ferromagnet, $\mathrm{H}=100 \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{m}$ Current to demagnetise the ferromagnet, $\mathrm{I}=?$
Using, $\mathrm{H}=\mathrm{nI}$
or, $\quad 100=10^5 \times \mathrm{I}$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{I}=\frac{100}{10^5}=1 \mathrm{~mA}$

question: The figure gives experimentally measured B vs. H variation in a ferromagnetic material. The retentivity, coercivity and saturation, respectively, of the material are:
solution:
x=retentivity
y=coercivity
z=saturationmagnetization

subject: Physics
q: Certain galvanometers have a fixed core made of non magnetic metallic material. The function of this metallic material is
question: Certain galvanometers have a fixed core made of non magnetic metallic material. The function of this metallic material is
options: to oscillate the coil in magnetic field for longer period of time
to bring the coil to rest quickly
to produce large deflecting torque on the coil
to make the magnetic field radial
solution: Certain galvanometers have a fixed core made of nonmagnetic metallic material. When the coil oscillates, the eddy currents generated in the core oppose the motion and bring the coil to rest quickly. Eddy currents are loops of electrical current induced within conductors by a changing magnetic field in the conductor according to Faraday’s law of induction. Eddy currents flow in closed loops within conductors, in planes perpendicular to the magnetic field.

subject: Physics
q: An aeroplane, with its wings spread <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> is flying at a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>180</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in a horizontal direction. The total intensity of earth's field at that part is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Wb</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> and the angle of dip is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo><mo>.</mo></math> The emf induced between the tips of the plane wings will be
question: An aeroplane, with its wings spread <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> is flying at a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>180</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> in a horizontal direction. The total intensity of earth's field at that part is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Wb</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> and the angle of dip is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo><mo>.</mo></math> The emf induced between the tips of the plane wings will be
solution:
∈=B→V→L→=BVLsinθ

=2.5×10-4 T180×518 m s-110 msin60°

=108.25×10-3 V

q: The mid points of two small magnetic dipoles of length d in end-on positions, are separated by a distance xx≫d. The magnitude of force between them is proportional to x−n where n is :
question: The mid points of two small magnetic dipoles of length d in end-on positions, are separated by a distance xx≫d. The magnitude of force between them is proportional to x−n where n is :
solution:
B = μ o 4 π 2 M x 3  for end-on position

U=− M → . B →

= μo 4π 2 M 2 x 3

F=− dU dx = −3μo 4π 2 M 2 x 4

therefore force between them ∝ x - 4

subject: Physics
q: A solenoid having area $A$ and length ' $l$ ' is filled with a material having relative permeability 2. The magnetic energy stored in the solenoid is :
question: A solenoid having area $A$ and length ' $l$ ' is filled with a material having relative permeability 2. The magnetic energy stored in the solenoid is :
solution: $\frac{\mathrm{U}}{\mathrm{V}}=\frac{\mathrm{B}^2}{2 \mu_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{u}_0} \Rightarrow \mathrm{U}=\frac{\mathrm{B}^2}{4 \mu_0} \mathrm{~V}=\frac{\mathrm{B}^2}{4 \mu_0} \mathrm{~A} \ell$

subject: Physics
q: Choose the correct option.
question: Choose the correct option.
options: True dip is not mathematically related to apparent dip.
True dip is less than apparent dip.
True dip is always greater than the apparent dip.
True dip is always equal to apparent dip.
solution:
If the apparent dip circle is at an angle α with true dip circle then,

tanϕ'>tanϕ ⇒ϕ'>ϕ

Therefore, apparent dip angle is greater than true dip angle.

Hence, true dip (ϕ) is less than apparent dip ϕ'

subject: Physics
q: The magnetic field of earth at the equator is approximately <math> <mrow> <mn>4</mn> <mo>×</mo> <mn>1</mn> <msup> <mn>0</mn> <mrow> <mo>-</mo> <mn>5</mn> </mrow> </msup> <mtext>T</mtext> </mrow> </math>. The radius of earth is <math> <mrow> <mtext>6.4</mtext> <mo>×</mo> <mn>1</mn> <msup> <mn>0</mn> <mn>6</mn> </msup> <mtext>m</mtext> </mrow> </math>. Then the dipole moment of the earth will be nearly of the order of :
question: The magnetic field of earth at the equator is approximately <math> <mrow> <mn>4</mn> <mo>×</mo> <mn>1</mn> <msup> <mn>0</mn> <mrow> <mo>-</mo> <mn>5</mn> </mrow> </msup> <mtext>T</mtext> </mrow> </math>. The radius of earth is <math> <mrow> <mtext>6.4</mtext> <mo>×</mo> <mn>1</mn> <msup> <mn>0</mn> <mn>6</mn> </msup> <mtext>m</mtext> </mrow> </math>. Then the dipole moment of the earth will be nearly of the order of :
solution:
B = μ 0 4 π · M d 3 = 1 0 - 7 × M 6.4 × 1 0 6 3

∴    4 × 1 0 - 5 = 1 0 - 7 × M 6.4 × 1 0 6 3

∴    M = 6.4 × 1 0 6 3 × 4 × 1 0 - 5 1 0 - 7

          = 6.4 3 × 1 0 1 8 × 4 × 1 0 2

          = 6.4 3 × 4 × 1 0 2 0

          = 1 . 0 4 8 × 1 0 2 3 ≈ 10 23  A m 2

subject: Physics
q: The coercivity of a magnet is $5 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{m}$. The amount of current required to be passed in a solenoid of length $30 \mathrm{~cm}$ and the number of turns 150 , so that the magnet gets demagnetised when inside the solenoid is _____A.
question: The coercivity of a magnet is $5 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{m}$. The amount of current required to be passed in a solenoid of length $30 \mathrm{~cm}$ and the number of turns 150 , so that the magnet gets demagnetised when inside the solenoid is _____A.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{\mu_{\mathrm{o}} \mathrm{ni}}{\mu_{\mathrm{o}}} \\ & 5 \times 10^3=\frac{150}{30} \times 100 \times \mathrm{i} \\ & \frac{50}{5}=\mathrm{i} \\ & \mathrm{I}=10\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A bar magnet is demagnetized by inserting it inside a solenoid of length <math><mn>0.2</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>100</mn></math> turns, and carrying a current of <math><mn>5.2</mn><mi> </mi><mi>A</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The coercivity of the bar magnet is:
question: A bar magnet is demagnetized by inserting it inside a solenoid of length <math><mn>0.2</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>100</mn></math> turns, and carrying a current of <math><mn>5.2</mn><mi> </mi><mi>A</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The coercivity of the bar magnet is:
solution:
Coercivity =nI=1000.2×5.2  A/m

=2600 A/m

subject: Physics
q: A paramagnetic sample shows a net magnetisation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>A</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>m</mi></math> when it is placed in an external magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>T</mi></math> at a temperature of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>K</mi></math>. When the sample is placed in an external magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>T</mi></math> at a temperature of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn><mo> </mo><mi>K</mi></math>, then the magnetisation will be :
question: A paramagnetic sample shows a net magnetisation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>A</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>m</mi></math> when it is placed in an external magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>T</mi></math> at a temperature of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>K</mi></math>. When the sample is placed in an external magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>T</mi></math> at a temperature of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn><mo> </mo><mi>K</mi></math>, then the magnetisation will be :
solution:
According to Curie's Law,
χ∝1T
Therefore, χ1T1=χ2T2
Also, χ=MB
So, 
M1T1B1=M2T2B2
On substituting the values,
6×40.4=M2×240.3

Therefore, M2=0.75 A m-1

subject: Physics
q: The susceptibility of a paramagnetic material is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>99</mn></math>. The permeability of the material in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Wb</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>, is [Permeability of free space <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>μ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>π</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>7</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Wb</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>]
question: The susceptibility of a paramagnetic material is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>99</mn></math>. The permeability of the material in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Wb</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>, is [Permeability of free space <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>μ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>π</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>7</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Wb</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>]
solution:
Using the relation of magnetic susceptibility χ and relative permeabilityμr.

μr=1+χ=1+99=100

Magnetic permeability, μ=μ0μr

=4π×10-7×100

μ=4π×10-5

subject: Physics
q: Two coils <math><mi>'</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi>'</mi></math> and <math><mi>'</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Q</mi><mi>'</mi></math> are separated by some distance. When a current of <math><mn>3</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> flows through coil <math><mi>'</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>'</mi></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> a magnetic flux of <math><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></math> passes through <math><mi>'</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">Q</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>'</mi></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> No current is passed through <math><mi>'</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">Q</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>'</mi></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> When no current passes through <math><mi>'</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi>'</mi></math> and a current of <math><mn>2</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> passes through <math><mi>'</mi><msup><mrow><mi>Q</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>'</mi></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> the flux through <math><mi>'</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi>'</mi></math> is:
question: Two coils <math><mi>'</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi>'</mi></math> and <math><mi>'</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Q</mi><mi>'</mi></math> are separated by some distance. When a current of <math><mn>3</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> flows through coil <math><mi>'</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>'</mi></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> a magnetic flux of <math><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></math> passes through <math><mi>'</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">Q</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>'</mi></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> No current is passed through <math><mi>'</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">Q</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>'</mi></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> When no current passes through <math><mi>'</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi>'</mi></math> and a current of <math><mn>2</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> passes through <math><mi>'</mi><msup><mrow><mi>Q</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>'</mi></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> the flux through <math><mi>'</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi>'</mi></math> is:
solution:
Since the coils are co-axial, the  flux through the coils can be written as the product of their surface area and the magnetic field produced by the coil along its axis.

Consider the flux passing through Q

ϕQ=μ0i1R22R2+x232×πR2=10-3 Wb

 

And the flux passing through P:
ϕP=μ0i1r22R2+x232×πR2

Taking the ratio
ϕPϕQ=i2i1×R2+x232R2+x232⇒ϕP10-3=i2i1
⇒ϕP10-3=23
∴ϕP=6.67×10-4 Wb

subject: Physics
q: In a uniform magnetic field, the magnetic needle has a magnetic moment <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>85</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> and moment of inertia <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>.</mo></math> If it performs <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> complete oscillations in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> seconds then the magnitude of the magnetic field is ____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mT</mi></math>  [Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open=""><mrow><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>85</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>
question: In a uniform magnetic field, the magnetic needle has a magnetic moment <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>85</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> and moment of inertia <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>.</mo></math> If it performs <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> complete oscillations in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> seconds then the magnitude of the magnetic field is ____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mT</mi></math>  [Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open=""><mrow><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>85</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
T=2πIMB
B=80×10-4=8 mT
